report_name: Lapsus$ Claims 4TB Data Theft from AI Firm Mercor Following LiteLLM Supply Chain Attack

threat_actors.name: Lapsus$
threat_actors.description: A notorious extortion group that claimed responsibility for the Mercor data breach, listing the company on its data leak site and alleging the theft of over 4 terabytes of data.

threat_actors.name: TeamPCP
threat_actors.description: The threat actor identified as responsible for compromising a maintainer's credentials and publishing malicious versions of the LiteLLM PyPI package.
threat_actors.goals: Compromise software supply chain to enable downstream attacks.

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Supply Chain
attack_patterns.description: The core of the attack involved injecting malicious code into the LiteLLM PyPI package by publishing malicious versions (1.82.7 and 1.82.8).

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts
attack_patterns.description: The threat actor TeamPCP used a compromised maintainer's credentials to gain publishing rights and publish malicious versions of the LiteLLM package to PyPI.

attack_patterns.name: Exfiltration Over Web Service
attack_patterns.description: Lapsus$ claimed the theft of 4TB of data from Mercor, implying the use of large-scale data exfiltration techniques, likely over standard web protocols.

attack_patterns.name: Content Injection
attack_patterns.description: Malicious code was injected into the legitimate open-source LiteLLM package to compromise downstream users.

malware.name: Malicious LiteLLM Package
malware.description: Malicious versions 1.82.7 and 1.82.8 of the LiteLLM PyPI package were published, containing malicious code designed to compromise systems that installed them.

tools.name: LiteLLM
tools.description: An open-source PyPI package that was compromised and used as a vehicle for a supply chain attack. Malicious versions 1.82.7 and 1.82.8 were published.

tools.name: Trivy
tools.description: A dependency used by Mercor in its CI/CD security scanning pipeline, which was reportedly the initial point of compromise in the attack chain.

identities.name: Mercor
identities.description: An AI recruiting startup that was the victim of a supply chain attack, resulting in a compromise and the alleged exfiltration of 4TB of data by the Lapsus$ group.

infrastructure.name: Lapsus$ Data Leak Site
infrastructure.description: A dark web site used by the Lapsus$ extortion group to list victims and publish stolen data. Mercor was listed on this site following the breach.

infrastructure.name: PyPI Package Registry
infrastructure.description: The public Python Package Index (PyPI) was used to host and distribute the malicious versions of the LiteLLM package for approximately 40 minutes.

indicators.name: Malicious LiteLLM Python Package Installation
indicators.pattern:
title: Malicious LiteLLM Python Package Installation
id: 2b1a9c8d-f3e4-4b5a-8c7d-6e5f4a3b2c1d
status: experimental
description: Detects the installation of specific malicious versions of the 'litellm' PyPI package.
references:
  - https://www.securityweek.com/mercor-hit-by-litellm-supply-chain-attack/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-02
tags:
  - attack.t1195.002
  - attack.initial_access
level: critical
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\pip.exe'
      - '\pip3.exe'
    CommandLine|contains:
      - 'install litellm==1.82.7'
      - 'install litellm==1.82.8'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the installation of malicious versions 1.82.7 or 1.82.8 of the LiteLLM PyPI package, which was the vector for the supply chain attack.

indicators.name: Potentially Malicious LiteLLM Version in requirements.txt
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Presence of requirements.txt for Review
id: 9e8d7c6b-5a4b-4c3d-2e1f-0a9b8c7d6e5f
status: experimental
description: Detects the presence of a requirements.txt file, which should be inspected for malicious LiteLLM versions 1.82.7 or 1.82.8.
references:
  - https://www.securityweek.com/mercor-hit-by-litellm-supply-chain-attack/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-02
tags:
  - attack.t1195.002
level: low
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|endswith: 'requirements.txt'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the presence of a requirements.txt file. This file should be reviewed to check for the presence of malicious LiteLLM versions 1.82.7 or 1.82.8.

relationships.source_name: Potentially Malicious LiteLLM Version in requirements.txt
